Urban drainage efficiency evaluation and flood simulation using integrated SWMM and terrain structural analysis
===============================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Xuelian Zhang a b, Aiqing Kang b, Xiaohui Lei b, Hao Wang a b

Show more

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2024.177442](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2024.177442 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0048969724075995&orderBeanReset=true)

Highlights
----------

*   •A method divides urban areas into high and low-risk drainage units using flood zones. 
*   •Corrected SWMM model simulates surface flood constrained by rainwater inlet capacity. 
*   •Overflow model ignoring inlet restrictions miscalculates infiltration excess flood. 
*   •Surface drainage crucial for efficiency; requires thoughtful design and management. 
*   •Identifying flood mechanisms boosts model accuracy and improves drainage management. 

Abstract
--------

A method for evaluating urban drainage system efficiency and simulating pluvial flooding is presented, incorporating rainwater inlet limitations through an integrated SWMM and terrain structural analysis (the corrected model). The corrected model is calibrated using data from two flood events and compared to an overflow model (non-corrected, without considering inlet restrictions) under the same conditions to assess its performance. The results show that the relative error of the flood peak in simulations ranges from 0.27% to 2.80%, while the Nash efficiency coefficient ranges from 0.3184 to 0.9504, indicating reasonable accuracy and significant comparability to the overflow model. Drainage efficiency evaluation reveals significant variation among drainage units, decreasing with increasing rainfall until surface depressions fill, after which surface drainage improves notably. This highlights the critical role of surface drainage in overall system efficiency and flood dynamics. Furthermore, two types of urban storm-induced floods are identified: infiltration excess and saturation excess floods. Modeling all floods as saturation excess may lead to substantial misestimations, stressing the need for accurate modeling that accounts for drainage unit characteristics. Understanding urban flood mechanisms is essential for improving modeling accuracy and optimizing drainage system design and flood management strategies.

2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0048969724075995-ga1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Introduction
------------

Urban drainage infrastructure consists of engineered systems designed to manage city rainwater runoff. Its main functions are collecting, transporting, controlling, and treating runoff from rainfall, snowmelt, and other precipitation. These systems are tailored to address challenges from urbanization, such as dense populations, impervious surfaces, and complex land use (Kalantari et al., 2019; Lourenço et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2019). Key components include rainwater management facilities such as green infrastructure like rain gardens, wetlands, retention ponds, and infiltration ditches, which collect, store, and treat runoff before it reaches natural water bodies (Sun et al., 2024; Eger et al., 2017). The infrastructure also includes a network of transportation systems—squares, streets, parking lots, underground conduits, and open channels—that convey runoff to discharge points like rivers and lakes. Additionally, flood control structures such as dikes, walls, gates, and pumping stations are strategically placed to prevent external floods and manage internal flows. Urban drainage systems also incorporate rainwater treatment and pollution control measures like sediment traps and filtration systems, ensuring public safety, health, and environmental resilience. However, urban drainage infrastructure is aging and increasingly inadequate to meet current and future demands (Berglund et al., 2020; Francisco et al., 2023; Larsen et al., 2016). Challenges include deteriorating structures, reduced reliability, higher maintenance costs, limited capacity, low energy efficiency, and vulnerability to natural disasters (Francisco et al., 2023; Boulos, 2017). Solutions require comprehensive infrastructure assessment (Byrne et al., 2017), strategic maintenance (Li et al., 2022), capacity expansion (Li et al., 2022), integrated water management (Bilalova et al., 2023), intelligent monitoring technologies (Zhu et al., 2021), and climate-resilient urban planning (Przestrzelska et al., 2024). Infrastructure assessment forms the cornerstone of effective urban drainage management and is crucial for identifying deteriorating areas, determining capacity constraints, and assessing vulnerabilities. Supports active maintenance measures, such as inspections, cleaning, repairs, and replacements, which help mitigate infrastructure aging and prevent urban floods.

Urban flooding has emerged as a significant long-term natural disaster for cities globally, with drainage system performance-related flooding being the most common (Bibi et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2021a; Zhou et al., 2019). Traditionally, inadequate drainage pipe capacity was considered the primary cause of urban floods. However, recent reports show that poor performance of urban rainwater inlets causes more flood events than capacity issues (Jang, 2023; Palla et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2021b). For example, in 2022, 78% of Guangzhou's flood events were due to inadequate rainwater outlet performance (Zhang et al., 2024). Urban rainwater inlets manage the exchange between surface and underground water flows but face hydraulic efficiency issues from blockages, aging, poor design, construction quality, and environmental changes. These issues prevent rainwater from entering the drainage system effectively, leading to surface flooding. The complex process of flow exchange between surface and underground water must be accurately described for reliable urban flood simulations and predictions (Rubinato et al., 2018). However, the diverse types and structures of rainwater inlets, their uncertain operational states, and random defects make precise descriptions challenging (Lv et al., 2024; Bazin et al., 2014). At the same time, recent urban initiatives like Sponge City (SCP), Low Impact Development (LID), and Green Infrastructure (GI) have made urban drainage systems, including rainwater inlets, more complex. This complexity underscores the urgent need for reliable methods to evaluate urban drainage infrastructure efficiency to reduce flood risks effectively.

Researchers and practitioners have used various methods to evaluate the efficiency of drainage infrastructure, typically categorized into four types. First, traditional manual inspection and visual evaluation provide valuable insights but cannot comprehensively assess system performance and understand detailed mechanisms (Halder and Afsari, 2023; Koch et al., 2015). Second, experimental studies based on structural or situational designs provide detailed insights into the performance of various drainage facilities. However, they are often constrained by time, space, and financial resources, limiting their broader application in management practices (Fathy et al., 2022; Hou et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021c; Zhao et al., 2023). Third, advanced sensing technology and data-driven algorithms enable real-time monitoring and analysis. These methods aid in identifying operational modes, detecting anomalies, and predicting potential failure points, facilitating proactive solutions. However, they are costly, rely on advanced technologies and complex algorithms, and require ongoing manual calibration as the technology evolves (Wang et al., 2021b; Eggimann et al., 2017). Fourth, integrating geographic information systems (GIS) with hydraulic modeling allows engineers to simulate scenarios and evaluate factors affecting drainage performance. While effective, hydraulic models are complex, requiring significant computational resources and frequent updates to reflect infrastructure changes (Fathy et al., 2022; Gülbaz and Kazezyilmaz-Alhan, 2017; Wang et al., 2021c; Zaman et al., 2021). Each approach offers distinct advantages while also facing specific limitations. The challenge lies in balancing practical constraints, such as time and cost, with the need for more detailed, accurate, and scalable evaluations.

This study presents a simulation model (the corrected model) based on the Storm Water Management Model (SWMM) and structured terrain analysis, designed to assess urban drainage efficiency and provide concise flood simulation data. This method falls under the fourth approach discussed earlier, which integrates GIS with hydraulic modeling (SWMM) to simulate flood scenarios and evaluate drainage infrastructure performance. Initially, GIS localizes drainage infrastructure with significant defects in the target area (i.e., infrastructure in surface waterlogged depressions and their upstream catchments). Subsequently, the drainage infrastructure within these areas is simplified into a set of facilities with equivalent efficiency based on urban runoff generation and concentration patterns. These improvements significantly reduce computational resource demands by focusing on a smaller study area with simplified drainage components and facilitating smoother infrastructure updates. Complex challenges such as pipe replacement, new drainage installations, surface greening, permeable paving, and potential blockages or leaks can be effectively managed in the model using drainage performance data (e.g., urban surface flood monitoring data), minimizing the need for extensive field measurements and adjustments to the model's visual objects and parameters. Finally, the SWMM model simulates urban surface flooding and evaluates drainage infrastructure efficiency. While SWMM is widely used for urban flood simulations, it has limitations, often requiring coupling with two-dimensional surface models for accurate flood depth and area estimations (Sañudo et al., 2020) and inadequately describing flow exchanges between streets and underground systems (Bazin et al., 2014). Our approach addresses these limitations by incorporating a virtual storage unit with an orifice and overflow weir, whether the ponding water is from surface runoff or junction overflow (Bazin et al., 2014). The model evaluates real-time drainage efficiency by comparing the water storage in the storage unit to cumulative rainfall during flood events. The storage unit's water level, area, and volume are provided as simplified urban flood simulation results. Verification with urban flood data from 2022 demonstrated high accuracy and reliability, indicating the model's applicability for studying urban flood mechanisms and forecasting. This approach avoids the complexities and massive workload of quantifying various structural forms, uncertain operational states, and dynamic changes at rainwater inlets. It operates efficiently with less reliance on precise terrain and drainage data, reducing the computational burden and making it suitable for evaluating large-scale urban drainage facilities.

The paper is structured as follows: The second section presents a case study in central Guangzhou, China, detailing data requirements, the model framework, and application steps. The third section focuses on the performance of the example model, calibrated using collected flood-measured data to ensure accuracy. In the fourth section, post-calibration, the model simulates drainage performance under various rainfall scenarios. It assesses flow exchanges between surface and underground drainage systems, evaluates drainage efficiency, simulates urban flooding, and analyzes the results in detail. The final section briefly summarizes the main conclusions and contributions of the research and discusses future work.

Study area
----------

The model case used for demonstration in the study is in Liede, a small watershed in the heart of Guangzhou, a megacity in southern China. The basin, part of the Pearl River system, covers 16.8 km 2, with a 6.5 km river and a gradient of 2.9‰. The north-to-south sloping terrain features upper mountain streams and lower tidal rivers, complicating flood formation and simulation due to the intricate flow dynamics. Verification
------------

The simulation results for three flood-prone areas using rainfall data from April 23rd and June 7th, 2022, are depicted in Fig. 8a–i. Relative errors for peak ponding water levels are F001: 1.25%, 0.89% (Fig. 8a and b); F002: 2.52%, 2.80% (Fig. 8c and d); F005: 0.47%, 0.27% (Fig. 8e and f). Nash efficiency coefficients, which measure the agreement between simulated and observed time series, are F001: 0.8838, 0.6537 (Fig. 8a and b); F002: 0.9504, 0.3184 (Fig. 8c and d); F005: 0.9035,

Results
-------

Fig. 10a–c illustrate the changes in drainage efficiency in flood-prone areas F001, F002, and F005 during various return periods. When the surface waterlogged depressions are not full (as shown in Fig. 10a and c), drainage efficiency declines as rainfall intensity increases. Once these depressions reach capacity, their water storage stops increasing. At this point, additional runoff is channeled to receiving waters via the underground drainage system or redirected to low-risk areas or nearby

Conclusions
-----------

This study presents a method for evaluating the efficiency of drainage systems and simulating urban pluvial flooding, considering the influence of urban rainwater inlet performance limitations on flood simulation. Flood-prone areas and their catchments are modeled as surface storage units with a bottom orifice, overflow weir, junction, underground conduit, and upstream subcatchment. Two example models with three flood-prone areas were used to validate the simulation of flooding and conduit

Cited by (4)
------------

*   ### [Impact of 2D and 3D factors on urban flooding: Spatial characteristics and interpretable analysis of drivers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0043135425004506)

2025, Water Research  Show abstract Urban flooding poses a serious threat to both the ecological environment and human society. Previous studies identified natural and anthropogenic factors as contributors to urban flooding, but little attention has been paid to the influence of urban horizontal and vertical factors. To address this gap, we conducted a comparative analysis of the patterns in spatial distribution of urban flooding in two megacities in eastern China (Beijing and Guangzhou). We then used Pearson's correlation to investigate the associations between flooding events and multiple influencing factors. Finally, two scenarios were designed to quantify the relative contributions of each driver using the Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) and Shapley (SHAP) interpretable models. The results show that: (1) urban flooding points in Guangzhou and Beijing are predominantly clustered in central areas, with mid-rise and high-density buildings presenting the highest flood risk. (2) in the base scenario, Annual precipitation (AP) is the primary influencing factor for urban flooding in both Beijing and Guangzhou. However, in the enhanced scenario, the addition of 2D and 3D (two-dimensional and three-dimensional) metrics shifts the main drivers to factors like Aggregation index (AI), Patch density (PD), and Building density (BD), significantly impacting urban flooding. This study highlights the critical impacts of horizontal and vertical urban structures and layouts, emphasizing the need for comprehensive urban planning and design strategies to effectively mitigate flood risk. It also provides new perspectives on urban flood risk management.    
*   ### [Mapping stormwater inundation potential in data-scarce urban areas: A machine learning approach for Kolkata, India](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212095525001671)

2025, Urban Climate  Show abstract Kolkata is a major fastest-growing city in India facing severe stormwater inundation yearly. However, it is difficult to identify stormwater inundation areas due to the unavailability of past inundation data on a large scale. This study focused on developing a methodology using Sentinel image and Machine Learning (ML) techniques for identifying inundation potential areas to minimize past inundation data availability problems. Stormwater inundation potential areas of the Kolkata Municipal Corporation (KMC) area are identified from Sentinel data derived inundation area locations and eight influencing factors using three ensemble Machine Learning (ML) models i.e. Random Forest (RF), Bagged CART and Extreme Gradient Boosting (EGB). The validation of these identified inundation potential areas is performed based on a separate stormwater event and field verification of inundated areas. The results show that the EGB (87.34%) outperforms RF (87.09%) and Bagged CART (84.46%) due to its advantage of adding a large number of weak models to improve the result. EGB indicates 30.1% of areas with high to very high inundation potential, especially in the western part adjacent to Hooghly River, the Eastern part adjacent to East Kolkata Wetland (EKW) and some areas of northern Kolkata. This higher potentiality is mainly due to topographic conditions, built-up density and age-old drainage network. This identified inundation potential areas will help the decision-makers to identify high-priority zones for developing plans to minimize this issue. This developed methodology will also help to identify the inundation potentiality of other inundation data-scare cities of the World.

URL: https://blog.iaac.net/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/image-586.png
DESCRIPTION: A series of diagrams and illustrations depicting innovative urban drainage and water management solutions, highlighting challenges related to capacity, maintenance, and sustainable design in aging city infrastructure.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Many urban drainage systems are decades old, leading to cracks, blockages, and inefficiencies, which significantly contribute to flooding incidents and the need for urgent upgrades.
As urbanization accelerates, existing drainage systems often lack the capacity to handle increased stormwater runoff, resulting in overwhelmed systems and frequent urban flooding.
Aging infrastructure poses maintenance challenges, with high costs and operational difficulties in maintaining and upgrading drainage systems, exacerbating the risk of flooding during heavy rain.
Rapid urban expansion leads to increased impervious surfaces, which outpaces the development of adequate drainage infrastructure, thereby increasing flood risks in newly developed areas.
